name: Divine Law (Halakha)
shortDescription: Halakha is the collective body of Jewish religious laws derived from the Written and Oral Torah. It encompasses a wide range of religious, ethical, and ritual obligations.
description: Halakha, often translated as "Jewish Law," is more accurately understood as "the way to walk" or "the path to follow." It represents the comprehensive system of religious rules and practices that govern Jewish life. Rooted in the Torah (the first five books of the Hebrew Bible), Halakha is further elaborated upon in the Talmud, a vast compilation of rabbinic discussions, interpretations, and legal rulings. The foundation of Halakha lies in the belief that God revealed his will to Moses at Mount Sinai, establishing a covenant between God and the Jewish people. This covenant includes both written laws (the Ten Commandments and other commandments in the Torah) and oral traditions that were later codified in the Mishnah and the Gemara (forming the Talmud). Halakha governs virtually every aspect of Jewish life, including dietary laws (kashrut), Sabbath observance, holiday celebrations, prayer, ritual purity, family law, and ethical conduct. Its purpose is to guide individuals and communities in living a life that is pleasing to God and consistent with Jewish values. The interpretation and application of Halakha have evolved over centuries through the work of rabbinic authorities, leading to different schools of thought and varying customs within the Jewish community. While there is a broad consensus on the fundamental principles of Halakha, specific rulings and practices may differ among Orthodox, Conservative, and Reform movements. The concept is integrally linked to the covenant with God, the revelation at Mount Sinai, and the role of Moses as the lawgiver. The Ark of the Covenant served as a physical representation of this sacred agreement and the tablets of law contained within. The pursuit of living according to Halakha is considered a central aspect of Jewish religious identity and spiritual growth.
